title: Just My Size Closet
summary: A child-height closet organizer with a divider, hanging rods, and adjustable shelves — built shallow from one sheet of plywood to fit little clothes and little arms.
description:
A standard closet hangs everything out of a small child's reach. This drops the rods and shelves to kid height so they can hang and grab their own clothes, which is the whole point. It's built about 12" deep — using 1x12 stock or a plywood sheet ripped into 11-1/2" strips — which suits children's hangers; go wider (a 1x16-equivalent rip) if you want it to take adult hangers too.

The carcass is straightforward pocket-hole casework: a center divider, two sides, a top, and a footer, with a bank of adjustable shelves on shelf pins on one side and a rod on the other. There's no back and no face frame, so don't rely on nails — pocket screws or 2" screws with glue are what hold it together, and squareness matters because nothing behind it will pull it back into line later.

Two things to sort before you cut: how you'll clear the closet's existing baseboard (the source just set the unit in front of it), and whether to drill the shelf-pin holes before assembly — much easier on a flat board than reaching inside a built box. For the rods, screw 1x4 cleats into the wall studs and hang the rod hardware off those and the side of the unit, so a loaded rod isn't relying on the plywood alone.
timeLabel: 5–7 hrs, plus install time in the closet

tools.note: There's no back or face frame — pocket screws (or 2" screws) and glue hold everything.
A track or straightedge helps rip clean plywood strips.
It gets fastened to the wall; plumb and level matter.
For the shelf-pin holes — a jig or spaced bit gives even holes.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Rip into 11-1/2" strips for the carcass. Or use 4 - 1x12 boards, 8 ft.

materials.name: 1x4 board, 8 ft
materials.note: Wall cleats for the closet rod.

cutList.note: Use 11.5" width if ripping from a plywood sheet.
Cut to 10-3/4" so they clear the pins and drop in easily.

steps.title: Plan for the baseboard, then build the divider
steps.body: Before anything, decide how you'll handle the closet's existing baseboard trim — the simplest answer is to set the unit in front of it. Then build the center divider assembly with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws (or 2" screws and glue). Skip nails; with no back or face frame there's nothing else holding this together.

steps.title: Drill shelf pins, then add the sides
steps.body: If you're using pins for the adjustable shelves, drill those holes now while the boards are flat and reachable — doing it after assembly is a reach-in nuisance. Then attach the two sides to the divider assembly, keeping everything square.

steps.title: Add the top and footer
steps.body: Fasten the top across the carcass, then the 1x3 footer at the bottom to tie the base together and give it a clean toe. Check the box is square across the diagonals before the glue sets.

steps.title: Install in the closet and hang the rod
steps.body: Set the unit in the closet, level and plumb, and fasten it to the wall studs. For the rod, screw 1x4 cleats into the studs on the wall side and hang the rod bracket off those and the side of the unit, so a loaded rod pulls on framing, not just plywood. Add adjustable shelves on the pins and any extra shelving above the rod to fit.

images.alt: Child-height closet organizer with a hanging rod and adjustable shelves
